name: Laozi
shortDescription: Laozi, meaning "Old Master," is a central figure in Taoism, traditionally credited with writing the Tao Te Ching. He is revered as a philosopher and a deity in religious Taoism.
description: Laozi, also known as Lao Tzu or Lao-Tze, is a pivotal figure in Chinese philosophy and religion. He is traditionally regarded as the author of the Tao Te Ching (also known as Laozi), the foundational text of philosophical and religious Taoism. While the historicity of Laozi is debated, his teachings and influence are undeniable. According to tradition, Laozi lived during the 6th century BCE, serving as a record keeper at the Zhou dynasty court. Disillusioned with the court's corruption, he is said to have journeyed westward, and at the request of a gatekeeper, wrote the Tao Te Ching before disappearing. In Taoist religion, Laozi is venerated as a deity, often identified as one of the Three Pure Ones, specifically Taishang Laojun (Lord Lao the Most High). He represents the embodiment of the Tao and is believed to manifest in various forms to guide humanity. Laozi's teachings emphasize living in harmony with the Tao (the Way), a universal principle governing all existence. He advocates for wu wei (non-action), spontaneity, simplicity, and naturalness. His philosophy has profoundly impacted Chinese culture, influencing art, politics, and spirituality. Laozi's legacy extends beyond Taoism, resonating with individuals seeking a deeper understanding of themselves and the universe. His teachings on balance, humility, and inner peace remain relevant in contemporary society.

sources.title: The Cambridge History of Ancient China
sources.author: Loewe, Michael and Shaughnessy, Edward L.
